steps.caption_md: Two independent reverse traversals, seeded from opposite boundaries: Pacific from the top row + left column, Atlantic from the bottom row + right column.
Walking uphill (`heights[neighbor] >= heights[current]`) from every Pacific seed reaches all 9 cells — this terrain rises steadily enough that every cell can drain to the Pacific.
The Atlantic traversal has reached everywhere except (0,0). Its neighbor (0,1), height 2, is already Atlantic-reachable.

predict.prompt_md: Can the Atlantic traversal extend from (0,1) (height 2) to (0,0) (height 1)? The rule moves to a neighbor when `heights[neighbor] >= heights[current]`.

choices.label_md: No — 1 is not >= 2, so (0,0) can never join the Atlantic-reachable set from this direction.
Yes — any cell adjacent to a reachable cell eventually joins every ocean's set.
Yes, because (0,0) is itself a Pacific seed, so it must be Atlantic-reachable too.

predict.explanation_md: The uphill rule is directional: `1 >= 2` is false, so the traversal can't step down to (0,0). (0,0) is a Pacific source (it drains directly into the Pacific by definition) but genuinely can't reach the Atlantic — the two reachable sets aren't required to match.

steps.caption_md: Intersecting the two reachable sets gives 8 cells — every cell except (0,0), which drains to the Pacific but is walled off from the Atlantic by higher neighbors on every side.
